sentences.text: Four and a half years in the ghetto.
I've never given up hope.
To the last minute I was hoping I would get over this.
If one gives up hope, that's the end of it.
A bit of each.
No, no, I didn't, we didn't-- if people tell you stories that they managed to pray, everybody prayed in their heart, I suppose.
But we didn't have time to pray.
I remember it was Yom Kippur, Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement that means in English, which is the most secret-- sacred day in the Jewish calendar.
And we-- and the Germans, they knew when the Jewish holidays are.
And they specially gave us harder work, and less food, and more beatings in those days.
And there were more killings going on in those days than any other day.
As a matter of fact, my mother and my sister were, were gassed on the first day of Rosh Hashanah.
I know that because I have, I still have three cousins living in Israel, and they were there with my mother.
And they know when they were gassed.
My sister maybe would have been alive.
Also she did the same thing as in the ghetto.
She went with my mother.
So they were both gassed.

And, talking about-- there were some exceptional people that kept-- we were 26 boys, I told you, living in this-- in the-- in the-- in the barrack, in like a, it was called a stube in German, a room with the-- we shared two, two boys to a bunk, well three bunks, one on top of the other.
And these bunks-- in the morning, there was a siren, and we had to jump off the beds.
And we had to make the beds, like in the army, you know, straight, the blankets and everything.
The blankets that we had, the one blanket, but it had to be nice and straight.
And there was one boy with us, and he found out in the next barrack there was a rabbi.
And he managed to smuggle in a pair of tefillin to the camp.
And he ran over every morning to this rabbi, just to put on the tefillin.
He was very religious, this boy.

And in the time that when he ran over to-- to-- to put on this tefillin, we took it turns to make his bed because otherwise he wouldn't have had the time to make the bed and run over there, because we had to be out within 15, 20 minutes.
I can't remember the exact time.
One morning we came-- one, one evening we came back from work.
I'm telling you this episode because you asked me these questions about religion.
One evening we came back from work.
He said, [GERMAN].
That means that the block fuhrer, the SS man, said the [GERMAN], room 18--
Step forward.
Step forward.
So we all stepped forward.
So he went back with us into the barrack.
And he asked who sleeps on this bed?
This bed wasn't made.
The boy that was supposed to make his bed, he slept in.
He somehow didn't make his bed.
So he pointed to this bed and he asked who sleeps in this bed?
So this boy stepped forward.
And he said, I sleep in this bed.
So he took us all out.
And this was in the middle of the winter, in it must have been December or January, 1945, or December 1944.
And he made us-- first of all, they got hold of this boy, and they started beating him up.
Somehow this boy survived.
He lives in England.
And to this day he can't walk properly.
He drags one of his legs from this beating that he received at that time.
What is his name?
He's Zalowski, Faiber Zalowski his name is.
And he-- and us, they made, they made us stand in the snow, in the frost, for three or four hours.
And they told us to have our heels up with our hands high.
You can't stand for long with the heels up for one or two minutes afterwards.
Every time we dropped our heels, we got a hiding with a rifle butt.
This went on for a few hours.
Then they let us go back into the-- I'm telling this story, this boy risked his life literally in order to be able to put on the tefillin every morning until this happened.
Then he didn't do it anymore.
But he did it for awhile.
And there were-- but on the whole, we were so absorbed trying to survive from one minute to the other, one didn't know what's happening from one minute to the other.
I mean, they used to come along every day and pick out the people that were weak, that looked weak or were weak, that didn't work properly.
They took them away.
You never saw them again.
And they brought new people.
They had plenty of people coming to supplement the people that they took away.
It was, it was, it wasn't a place where you were meant to be alive.
It was just a-- a temporary place.
And we didn't have time to think about religion.
But I never gave up hope.
I suppose, as I said before, because I had my brother.
It was a bit of a comfort to each other.
And anyway, to come back to this.
There was an uprising there once.
One of the-- see, the, the, the SS, they kept special people for the crematoriums.
You might have heard, they were called Sonderkommando.
And they changed these people every month.
After a few weeks, they killed off these people and they brought new people to look after the crematoriums.
So these people knew that their time is-- they had nothing to lose.
So one day they made an uprising.
In one of the crematoriums.
And they broke out.
But they were all caught.
Nobody escaped.
And that's-- when this happened, we were not-- we were told to go back quick into the barracks.
And we could hear a lot of shooting going on.
Also there was once that, and not far from us was a railway line.
And we had a lot of shooting with heavy guns as well.
And we heard later that the partisans blew up.
There was a train with arms traveling to the front line, and the Polish partisans blew up this train.
So they didn't let us go out to work.
When, if we needed a dentist, nobody took any notice.
We just had to bear and suffer.
But they, they were kapos, so they had special privileges.
So one day they were taken-- they said they need a dentist.
And the nearest dentist was in the main camp, in Auschwitz.
We were on the farm.
So they-- the only way they could get to Auschwitz was to travel with the wagon that brought the soup from the camp, from the main kitchen in the camp to us.
So every day this, this, this soup wagon traveled backwards and forwards.
So these two people went with the wagon.
And there were two SS guards with them on the wagon.
And they-- on the way they were stopped by an-- a car came, drove up and high ranking officers, German officers, went out of this car.
And they asked, who are these two people?
They asked the guards who are these people.
And he told them they're two prisoners that they're taking to the main camp to have their teeth seen to.
So they said, these high rank officers, they said, we want these two people.
We have to interview them.
And they took them away into the car.
And they drove away.
And when these guards got to the camp, they realized this was a plot.
They were, they were partisans.
And these two, they took away these two prisoners.
But later on the block fuhrer, he told us that they were caught, and they were hanged.
The partisans?
Whether it's true or not--
But the partisans killed the kapos
They didn't.
No.
They were trying to save these two kapos.
Yes.
One of them was a Pole.
And one of them was a Jew.
But they were both kapos.
They-- kapos had more contact with the outside world somehow.
They-- they had, as I said, more privileges.
They came in contact with other prisoners from other camps.
So maybe--
One was called Yanek.
The Pole was called Yanek.
The Jew, I can't remember his name.
But we were told by this block fuhrer.
He came, the next day he came-- or it wasn't the next day after.
About a week he came and he said, mm, you think they got away.
They were all caught, and they were all hanged.
Whether it's true or not, I don't know.
Maybe he said it to frighten us.
And that will [INAUDIBLE].
Anyway, when it came near the liquidation of Auschwitz, that was in January, 1945, we were told to start marching.
And we marched.
It was in January.
You can imagine the frost in Poland.
The snow was laying way deep, high.
And we started marching.
And we marched like this for three days and three nights without food.
Everybody, anybody who-- they were called-- they called them now the death marches.
You heard of the death marches.
This was one of the death marches.
Anybody that was left behind, that couldn't keep up with the marching, was shot on the spot.
There were bodies laying all along in the snow.
And the Allied planes, Russian planes-- I can't remember.
I think they were Russian planes-- came down low.
And they could see what's going on.
And the whole place was lit up around us at night with the bombs falling all over the place.
Thousands and thousands of people were on the move from Auschwitz.
It had, at the time, 70- 80,000 prisoners to be moved.
And everybody was on the way.
And finally we arrived to a, a school.
And there was an air raid.
So we-- they were frightened.
The SS were frightened for themselves, not for us.
They were frightened they would get killed.
So they pushed us into a school in the little town.
And we came into that school.
And we sat down on the floor.
And you know, when you're marching, and once you sit down, it's very hard to get up after you're tired out.
After a few hours, when the air raid was over, one of the SS men came in, an officer, and he said, everybody out, and we've got to start marching again.
But the SS themselves, they were exhausted as well.
The high ranking officers, they had cars.
They had-- they haul their horses.
And they were all right.
But the ordinary soldiers, they also didn't have-- they had some rations, army rations, I suppose, with them that we didn't have.
But they didn't have anything hot.
So they were also exhausted, so they didn't want to get up.
So he took out his revolver and went around.
He says, who doesn't want to get up?
And he said, if you don't get up and push us out, he will shoot them as well.
So we somehow got up.
And I-- I couldn't walk anymore.
I remember, I passed out and my brother, he-- he gave me some snow and he revived me.
And somehow I managed to walk on for another few kilometers till in the morning we arrived to a railway station.
There was a coal train standing there, an empty coal train.
It was full of snow.
The wagons were empty, but you could see it was black with coal, from coal.
So we had to empty the snow with our bare hands before we could get onto these trains, to these wagons.
And after a few hours, because they were  frightened  the Russians will catch up with us-- you see, the Russians were only a few kilometers behind.
So we started, the train started moving off.
And we moved like this, we traveled backwards of the train.
The-- the-- the stations were all bombed.
The lines were bombed.
And we went backwards and forwards and this and that.
Finally we arrived to a camp after a few days traveling.
The name of the camp was Gross-Rosen.
And we were waiting to get into this camp.
But we were told that the camp is full up.
There is no room for  us.
Luckily we didn't go into this camp.
So we traveled on till we finally came to Buchenwald.
And that was after about eight days, we arrived in Buchenwald.
We came into Buchenwald--
We started off, that train must have had about 40, 50 wagons.
Each wagon had about 100 people.
Usually it was a transport started off with about 4 or 5,000 people.
And a few hundred people only arrived.
Because on the way we stopped every day.
The bodies we threw off the trains.
And we were told to bury them near the, near the trains.
We dug graves and we buried the bodies.
And sometimes I used to wake up in the morning and I was laying on dead bodies.
The-- my-- my clothes was frozen to their clothes.
I had a job to get separated from-- from the dead people that I was laying on.
And when we arrived finally to Buchenwald, it was already like coming into, to paradise after this death march.
We came in and we were taken into a bath, a shower.
Didn't have bath, showers.
Buchenwald was a different type of camp.
You see, Auschwitz was an extermination camp.
They only kept the people alive in Auschwitz that they needed to work.
The rest were all gassed.
And Buchenwald, it was a concentration camp, where they kept people that worked in factories around-- there were a lot of factories around the camp.
And every day these people used to go out to work in these factories.
And also, there were not just Jews in this camp.
There were, from all nationalities, prisoners of war that did something wrong in their prisoners of war camps.
So they were taken out and sent to a concentration camp.
And Russian prisoners, there were a lot of Russian prisoners there.
There were political prisoners that were kept there.
For instance, I remember there was Leon Blum.
You might have heard of him.
He was a prime minister in France before the war.
Hm?
No, I never met him.
They kept, were kept in-- in-- in separate barracks.
The VIPs were kept in separate [INAUDIBLE] because they kept them in case they might have to exchange them for some other prisoners.
So these people were treated different.
But there were a lot of intellectuals, I would say, in-- in-- in this camp.
Anyway, we were taken out.
I was picked out again with a group of boys.
And we were put in one, in a separate barrack.
It was called barrack 6-- 66.
I think I, yes, I think I told you before that I was in the same barrack because I heard Elie Wiesel on the television.
He was describing to this Oprah Winfrey from America, he was describing there that he was in barrack 66.
So he must been together with me at the time there.
And I didn't know that he's Ellie Wiesel.
He wasn't Elie Wiesel then.
He was just a-- a skeleton like me.
And we were in this barrack.
My job was, it was to get up in the 3 o'clock in the morning and march for about a half hour to a railway station, and get on a train.
And we were taken into Weimar, which is a city not far from Buchenwald.
And our job was to go into the bombed houses and take out dead bodies, injured bodies, valuables, furniture.
Our-- our lives were expendable.
They didn't care if the house collapsed on us or not.
And so we had to risk our lives to get inside.
But we were happy to do that because by going into these places, we managed to find some food.
So we managed to smuggle home in the trousers.
We used to tie up the trousers at the bottom and put the food in the trousers.
And that's how we managed to smuggle home some food into the camp.
Either we ate it or we sold it for something else.
And that's how we managed to survive.
And this went on till April, the beginning of April, 1945.
When the Americans came near, we had bombing, and shelling, and sometimes we used to sit there.
If you lay on the bunks at night, then the whole barrack was shaking.
But we were pleased.
We could see something is going to happen.
And we were hoping we would survive.
But the-- when they-- they stopped giving food.
They stopped giving food into the camp.
And they said, whoever wants to get food should march to the gate, and there you will get a loaf of bread.
That was always the bait if you wanted something to eat.
So we decided-- we, when I say we, the boys in this barrack, we decided we're not going anywhere.
So we-- there was-- these barrack had a-- a-- a loft.
So we decided to get up onto the loft, as many as we could.
We tore away some of the boards, and we got over onto-- well, not all, but quite a few of us managed to get onto the loft.
And because we knew they would come into the barracks to drive us out.
So-- so many of us got onto the loft that the loft collapsed.
The whole thing collapsed.
So we were left in the barrack.
And when the Gestapo came in, the SS for the-- they started shooting.
So we had no choice, but we had to go out with them.
And we were taken away, pushed.
Thousands of people were pushed onto the-- the gate.
And there whoever could manage to get hold of a-- they were distributing bread there.
And I managed to get hold of a loaf of bread.
I don't think my brother managed to get-- anyway, they pushed us onto a, outside the camp.
And we were driven for-- for a mile or two miles, till we were put on the train.
And we started traveling.
We didn't know where we were going.
And we were on this train for four weeks without any food.
Now, you may ask, how did we survive?
I don't know.
Not many survived.
But we survived.
One night, I remember they said they were going to give soup.
So it was like-- like you won the lottery.
You know, they're going to get soup.
It came in the evening.
They started giving this soup.
And it was, I felt it was very salty, this soup.
But I was starving like a wild animal.
And we were-- so I ate some of it.
But something, I could smell something was wrong with that soup.
In the morning, there were a load of dead bodies laying.
The soup was poisoned.
They wanted to kill us off.
So they poisoned the soup.
And I'll tell you a little story about soup.
We were on the, on the, on this carriage.
It must have been about-- at the end maybe there were about 30 of us left from about 80 or 90.
The rest were dead.
So, and there were two guards.
On each carriage there were two SS guards.
So the SS guards, one-- one evening, it was at night, they got, they gave them soup.
And they also had such a container, a soup container.
So one of the SS men, he put this container down for a second.
And my brother, he got hold-- he was like a wild animal, my brother.
He was worse than me.
He got hold of this container.
And he rushed over to the other end of the carriage.
And within two seconds, there was nothing left in the container.
I-- I was with him, and we finished off this soup.
And that guard turned around, and he realized the container is gone.
And he was going mad.
He was also starving, hungry.
So he started shouting.
And he was looking, whoever pinched his container, he will kill him on the spot.
But he never found who-- he found the container, but it was empty.
That's how it was on that journey.
Anyway, we-- we-- we drove like this for four weeks.
And also back for-- why did it take four weeks?
Because the lines were bombed all the time.
I remember one day, we stopped under a bridge.
And there was another carriage, another train was next, on the next, on the next line was another train, an army train with German soldiers running away from the front line.
So we found out there was a carriage of food in that train.
So we-- there was an air raid on, so we broke open that carriage.
And we started robbing all-- the German SS were laying under the, under the-- the train because they were frightened of the-- the bombs.
We didn't care if we were bombed or not.
We just ripped open the carriage, and we got hold of whatever we-- I got hold of a basket of raw eggs.
And I vaulted over to our wagon.
And within two minutes, there's nothing left of the raw eggs.
We all finished them off, the broke and unbroken egg.
Anyway, this train stopped specially under that bridge because the Germans didn't want this bridge should be bombed.
So they knew that if the pilots would see us under the bridge, they won't bomb the bridge.
And they didn't bomb that bridge.
That's how they got saved as well, the SS.
Anyway, after about a day we were moved on further and further.
Then finally we arrived outside Theresienstadt.
